narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your low family income bracket, and we appreciate the full-time commitment you're ready to make to your studies. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and for you, it estimated a 48.3% chance of failure—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the program's success rates. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is well below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class and strongly pushed the model toward expecting bar exam struggles. Your LSAT score of 33, lower than the average of about 37, also played a big role but worked slightly in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing compared to even weaker scores. Interestingly, your low family income actually helped your case a bit, as the model sees students from similar backgrounds sometimes overcoming odds to succeed. Your Year 1 decile of 2 contributed toward the failure prediction, signaling early academic hurdles that persisted, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 had a smaller but negative influence in the same direction versus the typical 3.21 average. Being full-time, like most applicants, offered a minor positive boost since that's the norm for strong performers. Overall, your grades in later school years stood out as the top factor pulling the prediction downward, especially when stacked against peers who climb higher in their class deciles. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows room to strengthen in core academic metrics like deciles, LSAT, and GPA, where averages align more closely with success. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many from similar starts thrive with targeted growth. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic advising to improve future deciles, and exploring programs with strong support for first-generation or low-income students like yourself. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing, and reapply stronger next cycle. We're rooting for you.
